stories.headline: U.S. Inflation Surges in March, Driven by Record Energy Costs Tied to Iran Conflict
stories.summary:
Consumer prices rose 0.9% month-over-month in March, pushing the annual inflation rate to 3.3%, according to data released Friday by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. The spike was overwhelmingly driven by energy costs, with gasoline prices rising by a record 21% last month as the Iran conflict disrupted global oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

Core CPI, which excludes volatile food and energy categories, came in slightly below the 3.4% annual estimate, offering limited comfort to economists and policymakers. The data marks what analysts describe as potentially the fastest pace of price increases in nearly two years, reigniting concerns about the broader economic fallout from the U.S.-Iran war.

The inflation report adds pressure to ongoing ceasefire negotiations in Islamabad, where the U.S. and Iran are attempting to finalize a broader agreement to reopen the strait and stabilize global energy markets. Economists have warned that prolonged closure of the Hormuz chokepoint — through which roughly 20% of global oil and natural gas once passed — risks entrenching inflationary pressures well beyond the conflict's end.

White House officials have pointed to the ceasefire announcement as evidence that the administration is managing the economic fallout, while critics argue the administration's handling of the Iran conflict created the conditions for the price surge. Federal Reserve officials have yet to publicly comment on the March data.

stories.headline: Artemis II Crew Prepares for 'Fireball' Reentry After Historic Moon Mission
stories.summary:
NASA's Artemis II astronauts are preparing to return to Earth following a 10-day mission that took four crew members around the moon, the first crewed lunar flyby since the Apollo era. NASA has confirmed a projected return time and officials are describing the reentry as a high-intensity 'fireball' descent through Earth's atmosphere.

The crew received a call from a second world leader during the mission, according to live updates tracked by multiple news outlets. The mission represents a significant milestone in NASA's broader Artemis program, which aims to return humans to the lunar surface in coming years.

The splashdown will mark the conclusion of the first crewed deep-space mission in more than five decades and is expected to be closely watched by the global scientific community. Mission controllers have been monitoring spacecraft systems ahead of the critical reentry phase.

stories.headline: Hungary Heads to Pivotal Election as Orban's 16-Year Rule Faces Strongest Challenge Yet
stories.summary:
Hungary is set to hold a general election on April 12, with Prime Minister Viktor Orban facing the most significant electoral challenge of his 16-year tenure from opposition leader Peter Magyar. Magyar, a former insider who turned critic, has built a grassroots movement that polls suggest could break Orban's stranglehold on Hungarian politics.

Orban's Fidesz party has dominated Hungarian politics since 2010, overseeing sweeping changes to the country's judicial system, media landscape, and constitutional framework that critics say have eroded democratic norms. The European Union has repeatedly clashed with Budapest over rule-of-law concerns, and the election outcome carries implications for Hungary's relationship with both the EU and NATO.

Magyar's party, Tisza, has drawn support from urban voters and younger Hungarians disillusioned with Orban's government, though Fidesz retains strong backing in rural areas. International observers and democratic watchdog groups are closely monitoring the vote for signs of electoral fairness.

stories.headline: Meta Signs $21 Billion Cloud Deal with CoreWeave in Bid to Close AI Gap with Rivals
stories.summary:
Meta Platforms has finalized a fresh $21 billion agreement with cloud computing provider CoreWeave for additional computing capacity, as the social media giant accelerates its push to compete in the artificial intelligence race. The deal represents one of the largest cloud infrastructure commitments in the industry's history and signals Meta's determination to close what analysts have described as a gap with competitors including Microsoft, Google, and Amazon.

The announcement comes shortly after Meta unveiled its first AI model from a newly formed superintelligence team, underscoring the company's dual strategy of building out both hardware infrastructure and frontier AI research capabilities. CoreWeave, which specializes in GPU-based cloud computing, has become a key supplier to major AI developers seeking to scale model training and inference workloads.

The scale of the investment reflects broader trends in the AI industry, where leading technology companies are committing hundreds of billions of dollars to computing infrastructure in anticipation of surging demand. Critics and some investors have questioned whether the pace of AI infrastructure spending is sustainable given uncertainty over near-term monetization, while proponents argue the build-out is essential for maintaining competitive positioning.

stories.headline: Strait of Hormuz Remains Shut as Lebanon Fighting Strains U.S.-Iran Ceasefire Ahead of Islamabad Talks
stories.summary:
The Strait of Hormuz remained closed to tanker traffic on Friday as the fragile U.S.-Iran ceasefire showed mounting signs of strain, with both Washington and Tehran accusing each other of violations on the eve of their first direct peace talks in Islamabad. Iranian semiofficial news agencies published a chart from the Revolutionary Guard indicating the location of sea mines placed in the strait's main shipping lane, raising new questions about whether the waterway can be safely reopened during the two-week ceasefire window.

Israeli strikes in Lebanon that killed and wounded hundreds have added further pressure to the truce. The United States and Iran each characterized the Lebanon fighting as a ceasefire violation, while U.N. Secretary-General António Guterres issued unusually strong language, "unequivocally" condemning the Israeli strikes. The New York-based Soufan Center warned that the ceasefire "hovers on the verge of collapse," noting that Israel's actions risked being seen as escalatory even if Lebanon was technically outside the original deal's scope.

President Trump posted on Truth Social that U.S. warships and troops would remain in the region until the agreement is "fully complied with," warning that if it is not, the "Shootin' Starts, bigger, and better, and stronger than anyone has ever seen before." Trump also insisted Iran would not be permitted to develop nuclear weapons and that the Strait of Hormuz "WILL BE OPEN & SAFE."

The strait, through which roughly 20 percent of globally traded oil and natural gas once passed, remains at a near standstill, keeping energy markets on edge. The outcome of the Islamabad talks, the first direct negotiations between the two countries since the conflict began, is seen as a critical test of whether the ceasefire can hold and a broader agreement can be reached.

stories.headline: Ohio Man Becomes First Person Convicted Under New Federal Deepfakes Law
stories.summary:
A man in Ohio has become the first person in the United States to be convicted under newly enacted federal legislation targeting the non-consensual creation and distribution of deepfake images, according to reports. The case marks a significant legal milestone as Congress and law enforcement agencies have worked to establish criminal accountability for AI-generated synthetic media used to harm individuals.

The conviction comes amid a broader national debate over how to regulate artificial intelligence-generated content, particularly sexually explicit material produced without the subject's consent. Advocates for victims have long pushed for federal legislation after finding that existing state laws were inconsistent and often inadequate to address the scale and speed at which such content can spread online.

The new federal law was designed to fill a gap in the legal framework by establishing a clear criminal standard and federal jurisdiction, making it easier to prosecute cases that cross state lines or involve platforms operating nationally. Civil liberties groups have raised questions about how broadly such statutes might be applied, while supporters argue strong enforcement is necessary to deter abuse.

The identity of the defendant and specific details of the case were not fully available in initial reports, but the conviction is expected to signal to prosecutors nationwide that the federal law is an active enforcement tool and may encourage additional charges in pending investigations.

stories.headline: U.S. Jobs Report Shows Strong Rebound, Hiring Far Exceeds Forecasts
stories.summary:
The U.S. Labor Department reported Friday that hiring staged a significant rebound after the loss of 133,000 jobs in February, with new job gains coming in at roughly three times what economists had forecast. The stronger-than-expected report offered a measure of relief for markets and policymakers who had been watching for signs that the Iran conflict and associated energy disruptions were beginning to weigh heavily on the broader economy.

The report provided fresh data for an already volatile economic backdrop. Oil prices have surged and equity markets have swung sharply in recent weeks as the Strait of Hormuz closure throttled global energy supplies. The jobs data, however, suggested the domestic labor market retained more momentum than many analysts had anticipated heading into the spring.

White House National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett appeared on television Friday morning to highlight the figures, connecting them to what the administration characterized as positive progress on the Iran ceasefire and the prospect of the Strait reopening. Critics, including some economists and Democratic lawmakers, cautioned that a single strong month does not offset the structural risks posed by a prolonged disruption to global energy markets.

Markets had already rallied sharply earlier in the week following the announcement of a two-week ceasefire with Iran, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average surging more than 1,300 points on the news. Analysts said the combination of the ceasefire and the strong jobs report gave investors short-term confidence, though uncertainty over whether the ceasefire will hold continued to temper longer-term outlooks.

stories.headline: Iran Ceasefire 'On Verge of Collapse' as Hormuz Mines Remain and Trump Warns of Renewed Strikes
stories.summary:
The fragile two-week ceasefire between the United States and Iran faces mounting pressure, with the Soufan Center think tank warning Thursday that the agreement "hovers on the verge of collapse." Iranian semiofficial news agencies published a chart indicating that the Revolutionary Guard placed sea mines in the Strait of Hormuz's Traffic Separation Scheme during the conflict, raising questions about whether the waterway can be safely reopened. The Strait remains closed to tanker traffic, cutting off a passage through which roughly 20 percent of global oil and natural gas trade once flowed.

President Trump posted on Truth Social Thursday that U.S. warships and troops would remain in the region "until such time as the REAL AGREEMENT reached is fully complied with," adding that renewed military action would be "bigger, and better, and stronger than anyone has ever seen before" if the ceasefire collapses. Trump also reiterated that Iran would not be permitted to develop nuclear weapons and that the Strait of Hormuz "WILL BE OPEN & SAFE."

Adding further instability, Israeli strikes in Lebanon on Wednesday killed and injured hundreds of people, prompting U.N. Secretary-General António Guterres to "unequivocally" condemn the attacks. Analysts noted the strikes risked unraveling the broader ceasefire architecture, as Iran views Lebanese Hezbollah as a key proxy. The Soufan Center wrote that Israel's actions could "be understood both as an effort to drive a wedge between Iran and its proxies and as a response to being allegedly sidelined in the original ceasefire discussions."

Peace talks are scheduled to continue in Islamabad, but the combination of uncleared sea mines, ongoing proxy conflicts in Lebanon, and sharp rhetoric from both Washington and Tehran has left markets and diplomatic observers deeply uncertain about the durability of any agreement.

stories.headline: Melania Trump Denies Epstein Ties in Rare White House Statement, Calls Accusations 'Completely False'
stories.summary:
First Lady Melania Trump delivered an extraordinary statement from the White House Grand Foyer on Thursday, denying any personal ties to the late financier and convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and declaring that "the lies linking me with the disgraceful Jeffrey Epstein need to end today." Reading from a prepared text for approximately five minutes, she described accusations against her as "financially and politically motivated" smears and said she and her attorneys were fighting back against "unfound and baseless lies." She walked away without taking questions after concluding her remarks.

The first lady acknowledged writing a friendly email in October 2002 to Epstein's convicted accomplice Ghislaine Maxwell, signed "Love, Melania," though she did not address the email's content, which reportedly praised a flattering New York magazine profile of Epstein published that week. Melania Trump was also photographed with Epstein at a party at Mar-a-Lago in 2000. She stated Thursday that she was "not Epstein's victim" and had no knowledge of his crimes.

The statement created confusion within the White House over whether President Trump knew about its contents in advance. A spokesperson initially told the New York Times the president was aware she planned to make a statement, then later said it was unclear whether Trump knew the topic. Trump himself told a television host by phone that he did not "know anything about" the statement beforehand, adding simply, "She didn't know him."

Reaction split along familiar lines. Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene called the statement "brave," while Democrats used it to resurface questions about the administration's handling of the Epstein case. Epstein accusers Maria and Annie Farmer said in a subsequent statement: "What we want is accountability, transparency, and justice." Melania Trump also called on Congress to hold public hearings so survivors could testify and have their accounts entered into the congressional record.
